skill.name: site-architecture
skill.description: When the user wants to plan, map, or restructure their website's page hierarchy, navigation, URL structure, or internal linking. Also use when the user mentions "sitemap," "site map," "visual sitemap," "site structure," "page hierarchy," "information architecture," "IA," "navigation design," "URL structure," "breadcrumbs," "internal linking strategy," "website planning," "what pages do I need," "how should I organize my site," or "site navigation." Use this whenever someone is planning what pages a website should have and how they connect. NOT for XML sitemaps (that's technical SEO — see seo-audit). For SEO audits, see seo-audit. For structured data, see schema-markup.
skill.summary: Plan and design website page hierarchies, navigation structures, and URL patterns for better user experience and SEO.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: All 276 static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns (MD5 references, backticks, hardcoded URLs) appear only in markdown documentation and Mermaid diagram examples. This skill contains no executable code, scripts, or network operations. It provides pure documentation guidance for website architecture planning.

content.value_statement: Plan website page hierarchies and navigation that users can understand and search engines can crawl. This skill helps organize site architecture for better findability and SEO performance.
actual_capabilities: Design page hierarchies using ASCII tree format and visual Mermaid diagrams
Create navigation specifications for headers, footers, sidebars, and breadcrumbs
Define URL structure patterns that follow SEO best practices
Plan internal linking strategies including hub-and-spoke content models
Provide site-type templates for SaaS, e-commerce, documentation, and content sites
Generate site reorganization plans with before/after comparisons
limitations: Does not implement actual website code or CMS configuration
Cannot access existing websites to crawl current structures automatically
Does not handle technical SEO tasks like XML sitemaps or crawl errors (see seo-audit skill)
Requires manual input of business context, content inventory, and existing URLs

use_cases.title: Plan a New SaaS Website Structure
use_cases.description: Marketing teams planning a new SaaS product website need to define what pages to create, how to organize them hierarchically, and how users will navigate between sections. This skill provides templates and principles for creating intuitive site architectures that convert.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing manager or product marketer launching a new SaaS product

use_cases.title: Reorganize a Growing Website
use_cases.description: Content-heavy sites that have grown organically to 50+ pages often suffer from navigation confusion. Users cannot find content and important pages are buried too deep. This skill helps audit existing structures and redesign navigation following the 3-click rule and flat hierarchy principles.
use_cases.target_user: Web manager or content strategist at a company with an established website

use_cases.title: Design Site Navigation for Design Teams
use_cases.description: Product and design teams need visual sitemaps to inform wireframing and UI design work. This skill generates Mermaid diagrams and navigation specifications that design teams can use as blueprints for creating navigation components, menus, and user flows.
use_cases.target_user: Product designer or UX designer planning website navigation

prompt_templates.title: Basic Site Structure Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan the site architecture for a [site type] website. We offer [brief description of products/services]. Our main goals are [conversions/traffic/education].
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick request for guidance on structuring a new website

prompt_templates.title: Navigation Redesign
prompt_templates.prompt: Our website has [number] pages and users are having trouble finding content. The current navigation has [describe current nav structure]. Help us redesign the navigation following best practices.
prompt_templates.scenario: Diagnosing and fixing navigation issues on an existing site

prompt_templates.title: URL Structure Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: We need to define URL structure patterns for our [site type]. We are debating between [option A] vs [option B]. What URL patterns do you recommend and why?
prompt_templates.scenario: Making decisions about URL naming conventions and hierarchy

prompt_templates.title: Visual Sitemap Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a visual sitemap in Mermaid format for our [site type] website. Include these main sections: [list sections]. Show navigation zones and page relationships.
prompt_templates.scenario: Generating shareable diagrams for design teams and stakeholders

output_examples.input: Help me plan the site architecture for a SaaS marketing site. We have a homepage, features, pricing, blog, docs, and want to add integration pages and competitor comparison pages.
output_examples.output: The skill provides: (1) An ASCII tree showing the full page hierarchy with all pages organized under logical parents, (2) URL structure recommendations like /integrations/{name} and /vs/{competitor}, (3) Header navigation spec with 4-7 items prioritized, (4) Footer link organization into thematic columns, (5) Mermaid diagram showing page relationships and navigation zones, (6) Internal linking strategy recommending hub pages for integrations and comparisons sections.

output_examples.input: Our navigation is a mess with 20+ items in the header. Users can't find anything. Help us fix this.
output_examples.output: The skill analyzes the navigation problem and recommends: (1) Group related pages into dropdowns or mega menus to reduce header items to 4-7, (2) Move less-critical pages to footer or resource sections, (3) Provide a redesigned navigation spec showing the new structure, (4) Include before/after comparison ASCII trees, (5) Explain the 3-click rule and how the new structure improves findability.

output_examples.input: Create a visual sitemap for our SaaS product website that we can share with our design team.
output_examples.output: The skill generates a Mermaid graph TD diagram with: (1) Homepage at top with arrows to all main sections, (2) Subgraphs grouping pages by navigation zone (Header Nav, Footer Nav), (3) All major pages and their relationships shown visually, (4) Clear node labels with page names and URL paths, (5) The diagram is ready to render in any Mermaid-compatible tool for the design team.

best_practices: Follow the 3-click rule: important pages should be reachable within 3 clicks from the homepage
Use consistent URL patterns throughout the site: hyphens not underscores, lowercase only, descriptive not cryptic
Limit header navigation to 4-7 items to avoid decision paralysis; use mega menus or footers for secondary links
anti_patterns: Do not create orphan pages with no internal links pointing to them; every page needs at least one inbound link
Avoid burying important content 4+ levels deep without clear navigation paths
Do not mix URL patterns inconsistently (e.g., /features/analytics and /product/automation) - choose one parent term and stick to it

faq.question: What is the difference between site architecture and technical SEO?
faq.answer: Site architecture focuses on planning page hierarchies, navigation design, and URL structure for user experience and content organization. Technical SEO covers XML sitemaps, crawl errors, indexation, and structured data implementation. Use this skill for structure planning and the seo-audit skill for technical SEO issues.

faq.question: How many levels deep should my site hierarchy be?
faq.answer: Most sites work well with 2-3 levels of depth. E-commerce sites and large documentation may need 4 levels. The 3-click rule suggests users should reach any important page within 3 clicks from the homepage. Go as flat as possible while keeping navigation clean.

faq.question: Should I include dates in blog post URLs?
faq.answer: No. Dates in URLs (/blog/2024/01/15/post-title) make URLs unnecessarily long and add no SEO value. Use shorter patterns like /blog/post-title instead. This makes URLs more shareable and evergreen.

faq.question: What is the difference between ASCII trees and Mermaid diagrams?
faq.answer: ASCII trees use text characters to show hierarchy in a simple, portable format. Mermaid diagrams are visual graphs that can be rendered as images in compatible tools. Use ASCII for quick drafts and text-only contexts; use Mermaid for presentations, design team collaboration, or showing complex relationships with navigation zones.

faq.question: How do I handle URL changes when restructuring a site?
faq.answer: Every old URL that moves needs a 301 redirect to its new URL. Without redirects, you lose backlink equity and create broken pages for anyone who bookmarked or linked to the old URLs. Document all URL changes in a redirect map and implement redirects before launching the new structure.

faq.question: Can this skill help with programmatic SEO pages?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill can help plan how programmatic SEO pages (like hundreds of integration or comparison pages) fit into your site architecture. It recommends hub pages, URL structure, and internal linking for scaled content. For the content strategy and templates themselves, see the programmatic-seo skill.
